title: Reflection
text:
Building this ePortfolio has been one of the most practical learning experiences of my MSc journey so far. It pushed me beyond traditional coursework into the world of web development, version control, and reproducible data science — skills that are increasingly essential in modern analytics roles.






NoteKey Takeaway



The real value wasn’t just learning tools — it was learning how professional data scientists share their work with the world.

title: Reflection
text:
Building this ePortfolio has been one of the most practical learning experiences of my MSc journey so far. It pushed me beyond traditional coursework into the world of web development, version control, and reproducible data science — skills that are increasingly essential in modern analytics roles.






NoteKey Takeaway



The real value wasn’t just learning tools — it was learning how professional data scientists share their work with the world.

title: Reflection
text:
Git and Version Control
When I first encountered Git, I found the concept of commits, branches, and remote repositories genuinely confusing. The idea that every change could be tracked, reversed, or branched felt abstract compared to simply saving a Word document.
However, as I worked through this project, Git started to make sense:

Commits became checkpoints I could return to if something broke
Meaningful commit messages helped me track my own progress
GitHub Pages turned version control into a live publishing system

I now understand why the industry standard for any data or software work involves Git — it’s not just version control, it’s collaboration infrastructure.

title: Reflection
text:
Quarto and Web Publishing
Quarto was a revelation. Being able to write a single .qmd file that contains prose, Python code, tables, and diagrams — and have it render into a professional website — changes how I think about communicating analysis.
What I particularly appreciated:

Executable code chunks mean my charts are reproducible, not screenshots
Markdown + YAML is much more powerful than I expected
Bootswatch themes and SCSS let me customise without deep CSS knowledge

title: Reflection
text:
Python for Data Storytelling
Both of my featured projects — Customer Churn Prediction and Fraud Detection — taught me that model performance is only half the story. The other half is communication:

Converting Matplotlib charts to interactive Plotly visualisations transformed how readers engage with my findings
Writing code with code-fold: true lets non-technical stakeholders read the narrative while still offering full transparency
Adding Mermaid diagrams made model pipelines immediately understandable

title: Reflection
text:
Challenges Overcome







Challenge
How I Solved It




First Git push failures
Learned about Personal Access Tokens and branch configuration


404 errors on GitHub Pages
Discovered case-sensitivity issues in folder names


SCSS theme not applying
Fixed by correctly structuring scss:defaults and scss:rules sections


Missing frontmatter in project pages
Understood how YAML metadata drives the Quarto listing feature

title: Reflection
text:
Skills Acquired

Git and version control fundamentals
Quarto website publishing and theming
Python data visualisation with Plotly
SCSS styling and Bootswatch customisation
Markdown authoring (callouts, equations, diagrams)
Reproducible data storytelling
Advanced CI/CD pipelines (future goal)

title: Reflection
text:
Looking Forward
The skills from this module extend far beyond academic assignments. In my target career path — combining AI adoption, business transformation, and analytics consulting — the ability to publish reproducible, well-documented analysis is a competitive advantage.






TipGoing Forward



I plan to maintain this portfolio as a living document, adding new projects, refining existing ones, and using it as my primary professional showcase for graduate roles.


The combination of technical rigour (Python, ML, statistics) with communication craft (Quarto, visualisation, writing) is exactly the profile that modern analytics roles demand. This project helped me see — clearly — where my strengths are and where I want to keep growing.

title: Customer Churn Prediction for E-Commerce
text:
Overview
This group project builds a churn prediction pipeline for an e-commerce marketplace. The goal is to identify customers likely to churn in October so the CRM team can launch timely retention campaigns.






NoteDataset



286,208 transactional records from September 2025, reduced to 279,621 after outlier removal at the 99th percentile.

title: Customer Churn Prediction for E-Commerce
text:
Model Pipeline





flowchart LR
  A[Raw Transactions] --> B[Data Cleaning]
  B --> C[Outlier Removal 99th pct]
  C --> D[Aggregation to Customer Level]
  D --> E[RFM Feature Engineering]
  E --> F[Train/Holdout Split 80/20]
  F --> G[Logistic Regression]
  F --> H[Random Forest]
  F --> I[XGBoost]
  G --> J[Model Comparison]
  H --> J
  I --> J
  J --> K[XGBoost Selected AUC 0.79]

title: Customer Churn Prediction for E-Commerce
text:
Key Results






TipBest Model — XGBoost




Holdout AUC: 0.79
Recall: 0.77 at default threshold
Predicted churn rate: ~40% retained for maximum customer capture

title: Customer Churn Prediction for E-Commerce
text:
Model Performance Comparison


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go

models = ['Logistic Regression', 'Random Forest', 'XGBoost']
recall =    [0.65, 0.72, 0.76]
auc =       [0.76, 0.78, 0.79]
accuracy =  [0.72, 0.70, 0.68]
f1 =        [0.44, 0.45, 0.45]

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='Recall',   x=models, y=recall,   marker_color='#4361EE'))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='AUC',      x=models, y=auc,      marker_color='#7209B7'))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='Accuracy', x=models, y=accuracy, marker_color='#F72585'))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='F1 Score', x=models, y=f1,       marker_color='#4CC9F0'))

fig.update_layout(
    barmode='group',
    title='Model Performance Comparison',
    yaxis=dict(range=[0, 1], title='Score'),
    xaxis_title='Model',
    legend_title='Metric',
    template='plotly_white'
)
fig.show()


                                                
Model performance comparison across Recall, AUC, and Accuracy

title: Customer Churn Prediction for E-Commerce
text:
Feature Importance (XGBoost)


Code
import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

features = ['orders', 'Less_Active', 'Days_LastPurchase', 'sales',
            'Avg_ticket', 'FMCG', 'Low_ticket', 'Home',
            'Perc_discount', 'FinServices', 'Care&Kids',
            'Fashion', 'Avg_num_items', 'Electronics']

importance = [261.09, 131.71, 36.79, 33.56,
              21.96, 19.37, 16.73, 16.18,
              15.35, 14.05, 12.98,
              12.98, 12.74, 12.36]

df = pd.DataFrame({'Feature': features, 'Importance (Gain)': importance})
df = df.sort_values('Importance (Gain)', ascending=True)

fig = px.bar(df, x='Importance (Gain)', y='Feature', orientation='h',
             title='Top 14 Features — XGBoost (Gain)',
             color='Importance (Gain)',
             color_continuous_scale='Viridis')
fig.update_layout(template='plotly_white', showlegend=False)
fig.show()


                                                
Top 14 features driving churn prediction by XGBoost gain score

title: Customer Churn Prediction for E-Commerce
text:
Churn Rate by Business Unit


Code
import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

data = {
    'Business Unit': ['FinServices', 'Electronics', 'Home',
                      'Care&Kids', 'Fashion', 'FMCG'],
    'Churn Rate (%)': [27.1, 10.3, 9.7, 9.2, 9.1, 6.7]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data).sort_values('Churn Rate (%)', ascending=True)

fig = px.bar(df, x='Churn Rate (%)', y='Business Unit', orientation='h',
             title='Churn Rate by Macro Business Unit',
             color='Churn Rate (%)',
             color_continuous_scale='Reds')
fig.update_layout(template='plotly_white')
fig.show()


                                                
Churn rate varies significantly across product categories, with FinServices standing out at 27.1%

title: Customer Churn Prediction for E-Commerce
text:
Key Findings







Feature
Business Insight




Orders
Fewer orders = highest churn risk


Less Active
Customers already slowing down are most at risk


Days since last purchase
Long inactivity strongly correlates with churn


FinServices
27.1% churn — card distribution, not core e-commerce

title: Customer Churn Prediction for E-Commerce
text:
Recommendations






ImportantOperational Strategy



Run XGBoost weekly. Flag top 40% risk customers. Send high-risk/high-value segments personalised offers. Use low-cost nudges for high-risk/low-value. Monitor recall weekly — retrain if AUC drops more than 5%.


Team: Bolshakova Alina, Budhiraja Raunit, Lucio Pérez Wendy Monserrat, Thazhathethil Chandraprakash Rohith Krishna, Yalamanchi Vyshnavi

title: Rohith Krishna
text:
I’m a Business Analyst with hands-on Product Management experience, currently pursuing my MSc in Business Analytics at Dublin City University.
Over the past several years, I’ve worked as a Business Analyst and Scrum Product Owner, bridging the gap between business stakeholders and engineering teams — translating requirements into product roadmaps, owning backlogs, and delivering features that create measurable business value in Agile environments.
I’m now combining my business analysis and product management background with deeper expertise in analytics, machine learning, and strategy management through my MSc at DCU. My goal is to evolve into roles that sit at the intersection of data-driven decision-making and product strategy — where understanding users, designing solutions, and leveraging ML-powered insights come together.
This portfolio showcases my academic projects, technical capabilities, and the professional journey I’m building.

title: About Me
text:
Professional Background
Before starting my MSc, I worked extensively as a Business Analyst and Scrum Product Owner in fast-paced Agile environments. My work typically involved:






NoteCore Responsibilities




Gathering and analysing business requirements from cross-functional stakeholders
Owning and prioritising product backlogs in Scrum teams
Translating business needs into user stories, acceptance criteria, and sprint goals
Collaborating daily with developers, designers, and QA to deliver iterative value
Using data to validate product decisions and measure outcomes



This foundation gave me a practical understanding of how products are actually built — the trade-offs, the stakeholder dynamics, and the importance of clear communication. It also showed me where analytics and ML can elevate product decisions from intuition-based to evidence-based.

title: About Me
section: Why an MSc in Business Analytics
text:
Why an MSc in Business Analytics
After several years in business analysis and product ownership, I became increasingly aware of a gap in my toolkit: while I could facilitate decisions and translate between business and technical teams, I wanted to personally own the analytical and machine learning work that drives modern product strategy.
The MSc Business Analytics at DCU is where I’m building that capability — covering machine learning, statistical modelling, business transformation, new venture strategy, and AI ethics. I’m combining my prior experience with new technical depth to position myself for roles that go beyond traditional BA work.

title: Fraud Detection Analysis
text:
Overview
This individual project examines a fraud detection dataset to identify patterns distinguishing fraudulent from genuine transactions. Using statistical methods and predictive modelling, it delivers a fraud mitigation strategy for upper management.






NoteDataset



500 transactions, 23 variables. Target: is_fraud. Nearly balanced — 246 fraud cases (49.2%) vs 254 legitimate (50.8%).

title: Fraud Detection Analysis
text:
Analysis Pipeline





flowchart LR
  A[500 Transactions] --> B[Data Cleaning]
  B --> C[IQR Outlier Capping]
  C --> D[One-Hot Encoding]
  D --> E[75/25 Train-Test Split]
  E --> F[Z-Score & IQR Flagging]
  E --> G[Logistic Regression]
  E --> H[Random Forest 200 trees]
  F --> I[Insights]
  G --> I
  H --> I

title: Fraud Detection Analysis
text:
Fraud vs Non-Fraud Distribution


Code
import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Transaction Type': ['Non-Fraud (Class 0)', 'Fraud (Class 1)'],
    'Count': [254, 246]
})

fig = px.bar(df, x='Transaction Type', y='Count',
             title='Fraud vs Non-Fraud Distribution',
             color='Transaction Type',
             color_discrete_map={
                 'Non-Fraud (Class 0)': '#2980B9',
                 'Fraud (Class 1)': '#E74C3C'
             })
fig.update_layout(template='plotly_white', showlegend=False)
fig.show()


                                                
The dataset is balanced with 49.2% fraud and 50.8% legitimate transactions

title: Fraud Detection Analysis
text:
Model Performance


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go

metrics = ['Accuracy', 'Fraud Recall', 'Fraud Precision', 'ROC-AUC']
logistic = [0.68, 0.74, 0.65, 0.675]
random_forest = [0.70, 0.75, 0.68, 0.692]

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    name='Logistic Regression',
    x=metrics, y=logistic,
    marker_color='#2980B9'
))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    name='Random Forest',
    x=metrics, y=random_forest,
    marker_color='#8E44AD'
))

fig.update_layout(
    barmode='group',
    title='Logistic Regression vs Random Forest',
    yaxis=dict(range=[0.5, 0.85], title='Score'),
    template='plotly_white',
    legend_title='Model'
)
fig.show()


                                                
Random Forest outperforms Logistic Regression across all metrics

title: Fraud Detection Analysis
text:
Key Statistical Findings


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go

categories = ['Avg Age', 'Personal Income (k)', 'Credit Score', 'Avg Transaction ($)']
fraud_vals =     [51.7, 56.2, 645, 1850]
non_fraud_vals = [48.6, 64.1, 651, 2100]

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='Fraud',     x=categories, y=fraud_vals,     marker_color='#E74C3C'))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='Non-Fraud', x=categories, y=non_fraud_vals, marker_color='#2ECC71'))

fig.update_layout(
    barmode='group',
    title='Fraud vs Non-Fraud: Key Feature Comparison',
    template='plotly_white',
    legend_title='Class'
)
fig.show()


                                                
Comparison of key demographic and financial features between fraud and non-fraud groups

title: Fraud Detection Analysis
text:
Model Comparison Table



Metric
Logistic Regression
Random Forest




Accuracy
0.68
0.70


Fraud Recall
0.74
0.75


Fraud Precision
0.65
0.68


ROC-AUC
0.675
0.692









TipConclusion



Random Forest provides superior performance and is the preferred model for operational fraud detection. Fraud does not always appear in unusually large transactions — combining ML with statistical flagging (Z-score + IQR) gives the most robust detection approach.

title: Fraud Detection Analysis
text:
Recommendations

Use Random Forest as the primary detection model
Implement real-time transaction risk scoring using model probability outputs
Prioritise awareness campaigns for older and lower-income customer segments
Combine Z-score and IQR anomaly checks with ML predictions for a layered defence







NoteStatistical Note



The IQR outlier capping method applied boundaries at \(Q1 - 1.5 \times IQR\) (lower) and \(Q3 + 1.5 \times IQR\) (upper) to Transaction Amount, Fraud Amount, Loan Amount, and Income variables.
